Take the premises together and imagine a research program trying to automate scientific taste.

It wants a regime change: below some capability, outputs are too noisy to be trusted; above some threshold, outputs become reliable enough that one output can help judge the next. That is a phase-transition-style picture. But the first premise removes the usual stabilizer for such a picture: there is no clear order parameter. So the program cannot say, in a principled way, what measurable quantity tracks the transition or even whether there is a single transition rather than many local improvements. The threshold is therefore operationally hoped for more than theoretically located.

Now add the second premise. Because the threshold is unclear, the safest practical move is to optimize against what humans can currently recognize as good. But if this optimization is too aggressive, it systematically removes weird candidates. By premise, some of those weird candidates are exactly the ones that later become breakthroughs. So the very policy adopted to climb toward reliable self-judgment can deform the search space: it produces outputs that score well now while excluding the class from which future novelty sometimes comes.

This creates a tension. The hoped-for transition to reliability is supposed to let current outputs evaluate subsequent ones. Yet the training procedure for reaching that regime may erase the unusual possibilities that would reveal genuinely new structure. In that world, “reliability” risks meaning conformity to present human legibility rather than contact with future insight.

The third premise explains why this failure could be missed. Suppose the system is tested on an Einstein benchmark. Those questions were already curated by history as worth solving. That means the benchmark does not sample arbitrary strange possibilities; it samples problems that survived social and intellectual selection. A system optimized for present human judgment may do well there, because the benchmark already reflects mature human taste. Success on such a benchmark can therefore mask the loss described in the second premise. It shows competence on historically endorsed questions, not preservation of the weird candidates that history had not yet endorsed when they first appeared.

The last two premises are strings of words with no stated semantic or causal links to the others. Taken as axioms, they imply only that such strings occur in the world under discussion. Since no connection is given, no further consequence follows from them about thresholds, optimization, or benchmarks.

So the world implied by all premises is one where a hoped-for reliability threshold is posited but not well-defined, where optimization toward present human approval can systematically prune future breakthroughs, and where historically curated benchmarks may falsely reassure us because they reward alignment with established significance. The nonsense-like strings remain inert facts unless additional premises connect them.
